data.title: The $18 Trillion Bottleneck That Could Supercharge Your Portfolio
data.description:
Introduction

This year, I have spent a lot of energy (pun intended) on what has become what I call "My Favorite Place To Buy Stocks" in the title of my July 12 article, which was about the energy sector.

It's a super complex thesis for a wide number of reasons, including these:

  • Energy is critical in every single supply chain you can imagine. In the West, we owe our prosperity to affordable energy, as the Human Development Index comparison to energy per capita use below shows. There is not a single country on earth that has achieved prosperity without a steep acceleration in energy supply (to support rising demand).

  • Energy prices are usually drivers of inflation, which also explains why the holdings of the energy sector, in general, are the best companies to invest in during times of elevated inflation.

  • Currently, energy is one of the cheapest sectors with the highest yield in a market that is far from cheap and driven by a handful of Big Tech stocks. This was the main takeaway of my article last week, when I included a chart from Gavekal Research, which made the case that "Buying undervalued oil shares seems like a good bet".

Then, yesterday, I wrote what I consider to be one of my most important pieces "ever", when I explained how we could be looking at a prolonged period of elevated inflation, supported by government measures to prioritize nominal GDP growth over fighting inflation.

This, too, is super bullish for energy.

Having said all of this, I'm writing this article to update my thesis and shed some light on new developments that I could not highlight last week. I also want to point out something with regard to the bigger picture that I consider to be extremely important.

So, as this is about to become yet another long article, let's get right to it!

Oil, We'll Need It A Lot Longer

When I was a kid in the early 2000s, the anti-oil sentiment started to grow, fueled by environmentalists and expectations of "peak oil" supply.

Although I never supported the global call to ban oil, the "peak oil" supply headlines back then made sense. After all, between the 1970s and the early 2000s, the United States witnessed four decades of declining oil production, which started to create some wild headlines.

Interestingly, in the early 2000s, the shale revolution started. First, it caused natural gas production to skyrocket, followed by onshore oil in the years after the Great Financial Crisis. The result was a global oil glut that caused at least two major oil price crashes between 2013 and 2021.

Now, even the "peak demand" thesis is weakening.

Last week, The Wall Street Journal wrote a piece titled "Oil Age Is Far From Over, OPEC Says". While it's fair to say that the world's leading oil cartel is not entirely unbiased, they have made reasonable assumptions for decades, as they are not in the business of "pumping" the price of oil but carefully planning oil production to incorporate issues like global demand, domestic funding needs, geopolitical relations (i.e., OPEC vs. U.S. shale), and other issues.

The cartel is agreeing with my view that the global boost in middle-class wealth will require many decades of rising energy demand, benefiting oil and gas as well as alternatives like nuclear energy.

OPEC made the case that "oil demand will keep rising through mid-century and that there is no peak in sight". As a result, it sees demand rise from 103.7 million barrels per day in 2024 to almost 123 million daily barrels in 2050.

Moreover, the share of oil in the global energy mix is expected to hold almost 30%. When adding fast-growing natural gas demand, these two energy sources are expected to account for more than 50% of the energy supply, even more than two decades from now.

In light of ongoing global population growth, booming demand in emerging markets, which offsets higher energy efficiencies in the West (see above), artificial intelligence, and general electrification, there's no way renewable energy and nuclear can support rising demand without oil and gas.

The bulk of last year's energy demand growth came from surging electricity use, fueled by the rapid expansion of data centers and artificial intelligence, particularly in the U.S., Europe, and China, the cartel said.

OPEC's demand outlook hinges on expectations of robust population and economic growth. - WSJ

That's good.

However, it gets better.

Because "everyone" was convinced that renewables are the future, supported by government measures to push as much capital into future energy sources, oil and gas investments have been lagging.

After more than a decade of ultra-volatile oil prices, demand shocks, and negative sentiment from investors, energy companies have dialed down investments in production.

The chart from ExxonMobil (XOM) perfectly displays this, as it shows that, based on investments in existing fields, we will not be able to satisfy global oil demand. It won't even be close. Also, note that Exxon uses the IEA's global oil demand outlook, which is a lot more conservative than OPEC's latest outlook.

OPEC confirms this.

After reviewing its >300-page outlook, I found a paragraph that shows OPEC projects $18.2 trillion in total oil investments by 2050. The vast majority of this will need to go into upstream (oil production) to meet future demand.

As dramatic as it may sound, our inability to boost investments could lead to energy insecurity and elevated prices, as we simply don't have the favorable supply dynamics we enjoyed in the past anymore.

To reliably supply markets, against the backdrop of rising demand, as well as to offset natural decline in mature fields, global cumulative investments of $18.2 trillion are required over the 2025-2050 period (all in US$2025). - OPEC

This $18 trillion number is extremely important, as it comes with endless opportunities, especially because America's most efficient drillers are running out of Tier 1 reserves in the most favorable locations, which is very bullish for oil prices, after the shale revolution caused major supply headwinds.

EOG Resources "is basically out of Tier 1 Karnes [County, Texas] inventory" for oily Eagle Ford wells, Roth analyst Leo Mariani said. In the Permian Basin, "EOG may only have a few years left of Tier 1 Permian inventory." - Hart Energy

  • High and lasting energy demand will tremendously benefit companies that provide equipment and services to (global) oil producers. This includes SLB (SLB), Haliburton (HAL), and Helmerich & Payne (HP), among many others.
  • Oil producers will benefit from potentially much higher prices, as I have no doubt that investments in upstream will not be able to satisfy demand, at least not until a first energy price shock serves as a wake-up call. And even then, I wouldn't be too sure of it. Especially after this year's volatility, (private) oil producers might be reluctant to boost upstream CapEx.
  • Midstream companies with the right assets benefit from long-term demand growth, including Canadian players like Enbridge (ENB) and TC Energy (TRP), which operate in the Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin. However, this also includes ONEOK (OKE), Williams Companies (WMB), MPLX LP (MPLX), and others. Note that MPLX is an MLP, which issues a K-1 form.

These are just some ideas. It's not yet the end of the article, as I want to focus more on the attractive valuation of energy.

See, on top of favorable supply/demand dynamics, I believe energy is dirt-cheap, given the major bottleneck it has become.

A Dirt-Cheap Bottleneck

Stock market valuations are fascinating because there is no scientific way to determine if something is genuinely cheap or expensive. After all, even "expensive" companies can be attractive if they maintain high growth over time. Moreover, many "cheap" companies are cheap for a reason.

Right now, Nvidia (NVDA), one of the biggest AI beneficiaries, is trading at roughly $4 trillion, almost a trillion above the entire market cap of all German stock-listed companies combined.

If we dive a bit deeper, we find that at its current valuation, the semiconductor giant is worth more than five entire S&P 500 sectors, including consumer staples, energy, utilities, real estate, and materials.

I had to say that I am blown away by the fact that Nvidia alone is worth more than twice the combined value of all S&P 500 energy companies.

Please note that I am not bringing this up to "hate" Nvidia.

I am bringing this up because energy isn't just a cyclical sector but also the bottleneck of the AI revolution. Last year, I discussed this in an article titled "25% Of My Net Worth-Here's My Single Best Investment Idea".

Earlier this month, Bloomberg noted that electric utilities have asked for a rate increase that is more than twice as high as their request last year.

This is happening everywhere, as the power supply is simply overwhelmed:

The requested rate increases come as coal and natural gas plants are being retired, with not enough replacement sources being built. US President Donald Trump is seeking to slow those fossil-fuel closures while ending tax incentives for wind power and also for solar, the cheapest domestic electric source. - Bloomberg

By 2030, global data center power demand could rise from less than 300 TWh in 2020 to nearly 1,000 TWh in 2030, with some of the latest mega data centers consuming as much power as 5 million homes. This sentence does not include a typo. It's really that much.

As gas supplies 40% of the data center power demand in the U.S., demand for fossil fuels is getting tremendous support. In all honesty, I don't even think we are anywhere close to "peak coal", which makes me much more upbeat than even OPEC and would make stocks like Peabody Energy (BTU) and Core Natural Resources (CNX) great investments (but super volatile!).

That's why I fully agree with the words CSIS used when it wrote an article titled "The Electricity Supply Bottleneck on U.S. AI Dominance", which included the quote below:

The rise of AI has elevated electricity supply to a new level of strategic importance. A new long-term U.S. energy strategy should seek to establish global dominance in electricity supply comparable to the achieved global dominance in the oil and gas sector. The present reality of electricity scarcity that inhibits AI progress should be transformed into a long-term position of global dominance in electricity supply. - CSIS

EQT Corp. (EQT), one of America's largest natural gas producers, sees incremental natural gas demand growth from AI data centers of 10 billion cubic feet per day by 2030, a mind-blowing number by any standard.

This is one major reason why I expect a long-term rotation into energy stocks, as the trend of $18 trillion in expected CapEx shortages is massively supported by AI and the fact that Big Tech will have to spend aggressively on AI to remain competitive. Especially in this environment, laggards could be punished hard for not being on top of the latest AI trend.

Moreover, AI is working its way through the economy. For now, the main focus is on Big Tech. However, this will shift to AI-driven healthcare innovation, industrial optimization, and so much more.

Currently, energy accounts for less than 3.0% of the S&P 500. It's hard to see in the chart below, but energy is basically nonexistent in the index. During the Great Financial Crisis, energy accounted for more than 16.0% of the index.

Although Big Tech stocks absolutely deserve to be bigger due to their innovative power and elevated growth rates, I believe the fact that energy accounts for less than 3.0% of the market while information technology and communications services account for nearly 50% is "nuts".

In the coming decade or decades, I expect energy to gain a much larger share as the market recognizes that energy is not only essential and inexpensive (as I discussed in last week's article), but also the primary bottleneck for AI innovation. I also expect that the bottom in the energy-to-S&P 500 ratio (see below) of 2020 will last.

While I am not going to pretend that I know when the rotation from growth to value and general capital flows into energy will start, I am as sure as it gets that energy will outperform in the years ahead.

That's why I will continue to spend time on this sector and its many fantastic stocks, as I see value across the entire sector, from upstream producers like Diamondback Energy (FANG) and ExxonMobil to midstream companies like ONEOK and MPLX.

Another benefit is the ability to buy both growth and value, as midstream stocks usually yield between 5% and 8%, while stocks like FANG pay special dividends. Then, there are fast-growing Canadian companies like ARC Resources (ARX:CA) that come with elevated output growth and a 3% yield.

This diversity, on top of a strong thesis, is one of the reasons why I never run out of ideas to discuss.

Takeaway

Energy isn't just another sector. I think it's fair to call it the backbone of global prosperity, the hidden driver of inflation resilience, and now, the bottleneck of the AI revolution.

Despite a ton of negative sentiment for decades, demand for oil and gas isn't disappearing. On the contrary, it's accelerating.

OPEC expects $18 trillion in upstream investments will be needed by 2050 just to keep up, and AI's explosive power demands are only tightening the squeeze.

Yet, energy stocks remain absurdly cheap, trading at a fraction of Big Tech's valuations while serving as the critical infrastructure for everything from data centers to emerging markets.

While the sector's weight in the S&P 500 is near historic lows, the math is undeniable. Either energy catches a bid or the world faces a supply crisis.

Either way, energy should be a fantastic place to be, which is why I'm doubling down before the sector becomes popular again.

Risks To My Thesis

A steep recession is the biggest supply risk that could derail the thesis. Structural risks include prolonged supply growth from OPEC, short-term supply growth in Brazil and Guyana, and a faster adoption of nuclear energy.

However, given the bigger picture and supply constraints even in OPEC and South American nations, I believe the risk/reward of my thesis remains solid.

Nonetheless, as bullish as I may be, please be aware that energy, in general, is very volatile. While I may be overweight in this sector, it may not fit your risk profile.

data.title: Most Managed Care Insurers Expected To See Q2 2025 Drops In Revenue, Net Income
data.description:
A majority of publicly traded US health insurers are expected to post quarter-over-quarter declines in revenue and net income in the second quarter, according to an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis of sell-side analyst forecasts.

Of the eight largest publicly traded US managed care insurers, all but two — UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) and Alignment Healthcare Inc. (ALHC) — are projected to report lower revenue compared with the first quarter of 2025, while all eight are forecast to log higher revenue than the second quarter of 2024.

Costs, uncertainty mount

Over the last few years, high costs associated with the senior-aimed Medicare Advantage plans and changes to Medicaid membership have complicated earnings figures for major managed care insurers, a trend that has continued to now, according to James Sung, director of insurance ratings for S&P Global Ratings.

In July, Centene Corp. (CNC) joined UnitedHealth in pulling its 2025 earnings guidance, with the magnitude of its earnings misestimation and risk adjustment proving to be a surprise, Sung said.

"I think some of the large companies pulling guidance partially provides some cover for the other companies to be a little bit more conservative with the guidance," Sung said. "We might see this play out for the other companies, but since first quarter earnings, a lot of companies have already kind of hinted at some pressures."

The pressures already facing Medicaid are likely to be further complicated by President Donald Trump's budget bill, also referred to as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which cuts some federal support for the subsidized health plans aimed at low-income individuals.

Health insurers still face challenges related to the post-COVID-19 resumption of redetermination, the process by which states decide who qualifies for Medicaid, and the shift toward a sicker, more costly membership mix that followed.

The legislation includes a requirement that states redetermine the eligibility of Medicaid expansion members at least twice a year and adds work requirements for the Medicaid expansion population, among other changes, according to a report from S&P Global Ratings.

Even with all these changes set to go into effect in the coming years, the full impact may not be commented on for several quarters, Sung said.

"A lot of the Medicaid cuts and changes, like adding work requirements and a double redetermination, won't start until maybe 2027, at the earliest," Sung said. "A lot of it is delayed and creates an extended overhang for the sector that people will be monitoring and asking about every quarter."

Net income drops

In the second quarter, all eight of the largest publicly traded US managed care insurers, except The Cigna Group (CI), are expected to log lower net income sequentially, while all eight are forecast to report lower net income year-over-year.

Second quarter earnings will not put recent debates or investor concerns to bed but could provide an opportunity for some approximation of clarity, J.P. Morgan analysts Lisa Gill, John Stansel, Benjamin Rossi and Cole Harris wrote in a July 13 research note.

"Across the group, we believe expectations are low with the potential to see some better-than-feared quarters," the analysts wrote. "Overall, our near-term bias remains towards [Cigna] and CVS (CVS) with cleaner stories with less exposure to government insurance and larger healthcare services businesses, where we feel comfortable that guidance is attainable."

Medical losses climb

Analysts additionally expect medical-loss ratio deterioration for all but one publicly traded US managed care insurer in the second quarter.

All but Molina Healthcare Inc. (MOH) are expected to see their medical loss ratios rise from the first quarter. Medical loss ratios for seven of the eight are also projected to increase year-over-year. The eighth company, Alignment, is not expected to see a change in medical loss ratio year-over-year.

In previous quarters, various insurers have pointed to cost pressures from the senior-aimed Medicare Advantage plans, specifically citing care delayed by COVID-19 as a factor.

However, recent changes, including rate adjustments, may help offset this impact, the J.P. Morgan analysts wrote.

"Most [managed care insurers] have indicated to date that [Medicare Advantage] is performing in line with or better than expectations, a positive after a challenging 2024, with minimal updates to guidance in [the first quarter] out of an abundance of caution," the analysts wrote. "With minimal shifts in enrollment [quarter over quarter], we think that [the second quarter] provides a relatively consistent baseline and opportunity for [managed care insurers] to frame whether utilization has continued to progress in line with expectations."

The second quarter managed care earnings season will begin July 17, with Elevance Health Inc. (ELV) presenting its figures at 8:30 a.m. ET.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: 3 'Sleep Well At Night' Picks For Mom
data.description:
This article was co-produced with Kody Kester.

Achieving long-term investing success doesn't have to be overly complex. We would argue that Warren Buffett's philosophy of emphasizing simplicity (e.g., KISS or Keep It Simple, Stupid investing) is a sound approach.

Our approach to investing is as follows:

  1. Look for businesses with wide moats. These are companies with operations that can't be easily upstaged by established competitors or new upstarts.
  2. Look for businesses that are operating in industries with tangible growth catalysts. Some of the most powerful ongoing growth stories include AI software, cloud services, semiconductors, and data centers.
  3. Seek out companies with durable, investment-grade balance sheets. This allows them access to cheaper capital to fund growth projects and can position them to withstand a temporary downturn.
  4. We like to see companies with low dividend payout ratios, too. Along with the other attributes, this tends to result in generous and sustainable dividend growth over time.
  5. Finally, we prefer to buy businesses trading below our fair value estimates. This can help to limit the downside if risks play out. Such an approach can also maximize upside potential.

Without further ado, let's dive into three picks that we believe possess each of the preferred attributes.

Salesforce (CRM): A Bargain For A Cloud CRM Titan

The business world is very customer-centric.

Short of a company being the only option for a customer, it's a safe bet that if a customer is unhappy, they will be looking to other businesses to fulfill their needs. That's why satisfied customers are paramount to whether most businesses survive, let alone thrive.

This underscores the importance of customer relationship management software. That's where our first pick of the day, Salesforce, shines. Since its founding in 1999, CRM has risen to become the leading AI CRM software company.

The company's AI-powered Salesforce Platform helps customers to handle many functions more smoothly, including marketing, sales, and customer service. CRM's recently launched Agentforce Platform helps customers augment their teams with autonomous AI agents that function 24/7. These platforms can assist businesses in operating efficiently while also keeping customers happy.

CRM serves more than 150,000 customers in dozens of countries around the world on a subscription basis. These range from startups to Fortune 500 companies in a wide variety of industries.

CRM's status as the leader in a large market is a notable positive. Better yet, this market is set to get bigger in the years ahead.

Mordor Intelligence anticipates that the global CRM software market is going to compound by 8.7% annually from $81.2 billion in 2025 to $123.2 billion by 2030. The research firm credits growing cloud deployments and the shift to data-driven personalization as instrumental to this promising outlook for the industry.

As a result, we think the FAST Graphs analyst consensus for 12.4% annual operating cash flow per share growth through January 2028 for CRM is reasonable. This would take OCF per share from a base of $13.44 in FY 2025 to $18.98 in FY 2028.

CRM's 0.6% dividend yield comes in at about half of the S&P 500 index's (SP500) 1.2% yield. The company makes up for this lower starting yield with a payout ratio that's poised to be in the low to mid-teens for FY 2026. That's why we're confident CRM can build on its 1-year dividend growth streak in the future.

The company also boasts a rock-solid balance sheet, with an A+ credit rating from S&P on a stable outlook.

In our view, CRM's attractive valuation is what puts the buy case over the top. Shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/OCF ratio of 16.4 (54% weight for FY 2026 and 46% for FY 2027).

According to data from FAST Graphs, this is well below the 10-year average P/OCF ratio of 30.4. Compared to the current $256 share price (as of July 16th, 2025), CRM's shares are trading at a 22% discount to our fair value P/OCF ratio of 21 ($329 fair value per share estimate).

If the company matches the growth forecast and the stock returns to fair value, it could have a 27% cumulative total return ahead through June 2026 and a 15% annual total return rate by the end of FY 2031.

ASML Holding N.V. (ASML): Making The Ongoing AI Revolution Possible

The world runs on semiconductor chips.

Using a smartphone?

That wouldn't be possible without the chips that go into it.

Working on a laptop?

This, too, wouldn't happen without chips.

Using home appliances?

These products wouldn't function without chips, either.

Chips touch just about every aspect of our lives. And none of this would be a reality without ASML's extreme ultraviolet (EUV) lithography machines. The blending of this hardware with its software helps to produce the highly sophisticated chips that support our modern lifestyles.

One factor that bodes well for ASML is that increasing smartphone, automotive electronics, and AI adoption will raise overall chip demand over time. Another element is that, as the uses for chips become more extensive and advanced, so will the need for the company's hardware and software.

This explains why Coherent Market Insights is predicting that the global semiconductor equipment market will compound by 9.5% annually from $105.3 billion in 2025 to $198.9 billion in 2032.

Holding a virtual monopoly in the EUV lithography machine market, ASML is uniquely positioned to cash in on this strong market outlook. That accounts for the FAST Graphs analyst consensus that EPS will grow by 19.1% annually off the 2024 base of $20, reaching $36.93 in 2027.

ASML's 1.1% dividend yield nearly matches the S&P 500 index's yield. What's especially appealing is that the dividend has a lot of room for future growth. This is evidenced by a payout ratio that's poised to be in the mid-30% range for 2025. That is why we're optimistic that ASML will extend its 9-year dividend growth streak.

The company also enjoys an A credit rating from Moody's with a positive outlook.

ASML's valuation is reasonably interesting right now, too.

After the pullback today, shares are trading at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of 25.3 (46% weight for 2025 and 54% for 2026).

At the present $743 share price, this is well below the 10-year average P/E ratio of 34.8 per FAST Graphs and 13% under our fair value P/E ratio of 29 ($852 fair value per share estimate).

If ASML meets growth expectations, it could have a 15% upside through June 2026 (the FAST Graphs above don't yet reflect the current share price) and a 14% annual total return potential by the end of 2030.

Equinix (EQIX): Well-Positioned For The Massive Digital Infrastructure Wave

Equinix is one of the largest publicly traded REITs and is a way to play AI on the infrastructure side of the equation. Customers turn to EQIX for a few reasons, like it not being practical to own multi-tenant facilities and to minimize capital at risk in new locations. The company's 99.999%+ uptime record plays a role, too.

EQIX's 260 data centers (167 owned) serve over 10,000 customers across more than 486,000 interconnections. These are located on six continents and in 35 countries.

As one would expect from a company of its size, EQIX is highly diversified. As of Q1 2025, its top 10 tenants made up just 16.7% of its monthly recurring revenue, and the top 50 comprise 36.7% of MRR.

The mission-critical nature of this infrastructure also allows EQIX to embed annual price increases of between 2% and 5%. These contracts are also quite lengthy, with an average lease maturity of more than 18 years, including extensions.

Increasing adoption of technologies like AI, machine learning, and the Internet of Things are positive tailwinds for the global data center market. That explains why Grand View Research thinks that the industry can grow by 11.2% annually from $347.6 billion in 2024 to $652 billion in 2030.

Consequently, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for EQIX's AFFO per share to increase by 6.8% annually from a 2024 base of $35.02 to $43.02 in 2027.

While low for a REIT, EQIX's 2.5% dividend yield comes in at about double the broader market. The company is also set to pair this with at least 8% annual dividend growth from 2025 to 2029. This is due to a payout ratio that's positioned to be around 50% in 2025. That should allow EQIX to lengthen its 10-year dividend growth streak.

The REIT also has an exceptional balance sheet, with $7.6 billion in liquidity as of March 31st, 2025. The net leverage ratio was very robust at just 3.4x. These traits afford it a BBB credit rating from S&P with a stable outlook.

EQIX's forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio is 19.5 (46% weight for 2025 and 54% for 2026). That's well under the 10-year average P/E ratio of 24, per FAST Graphs.

From the current $760 share price, EQIX's shares are priced 12% below our fair value P/AFFO ratio of 22 (fair value per share of $859).

If the company grows as predicted and reverts to fair value, it could have a 15% cumulative upside by the end of June 2026 and an 11% annual total return potential through 2030 (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was from Equinix's May 2025 Investor Presentation).

Risks To Consider

CRM, ASML, and EQIX possess encouraging fundamentals. If key risks play out, though, that could materially alter the case for each.

Key risks for CRM include a competitive market environment and the potential for data breaches.

For it to remain successful, the company will have to navigate competition from the likes of Microsoft (MSFT) and Oracle (ORCL). If CRM can't attract/retain highly talented employees and continue to adapt to evolving customer preferences, it could risk losing market share. That could slow its growth potential.

CRM is also vulnerable to data breaches, with approximately 45% of data breaches happening in the cloud. If large amounts of sensitive customer data are compromised in a data breach, this could damage the company's reputation. That could result in lawsuits and potential regulatory fines, hurting CRM's reputation.

Noteworthy risks to ASML include a lack of visibility from geopolitical uncertainty and semiconductor cyclicality.

ASML is preparing for growth in 2026. However, this is up in the air. That's because macroeconomic and geopolitical developments from the ongoing trade war caused ASML to indicate that it can't confirm growth for next year. If headwinds persist for the foreseeable future, that could pressure the company's growth prospects.

Not to mention that the semiconductor industry is known for being highly cyclical. A slowdown in economic growth could lead key customers to lower capex, which could result in a slowdown in bookings for ASML.

EQIX faces key risks like an oversupply of third-party data centers and the possibility of setbacks to expansion plans.

A glut of third-party data centers could weigh on the company via lower leasing prices and reduced revenue. This could upend EQIX's growth story.

Additionally, the REIT could also encounter supply chain constraints on growth projects. If this happens, EQIX's projects could run into cost overages and time delays. That could also impede the company's ability to hit its growth targets.

Summary: Own The Future With These 3 Industry Leaders

We believe CRM, ASML, and EQIX are each high-quality picks. CRM and EQIX are rated as 12/13 Super SWANs in the Zen Research Terminal, whereas ASML is classified as a 13/13 Ultra SWAN. We believe these companies can capitalize on radically transformational technological trends, which could redefine the global economy for a generation.

The three come with a weighted dividend yield of 1.4%, which is marginally above the S&P 500 index's 1.2% yield. More importantly, their average payout ratio is in the low 30% range.

For more context, that's well under the mid-60% payout ratio that rating agencies desire from their respective industries, according to the Zen Research Terminal. This provides each with a runway to generously grow their dividends over the long haul.

CRM, ASML, and EQIX also boast investment-grade credit ratings, varying from BBB for EQIX to A+ for CRM. This translates into a weighted 30-year bankruptcy risk of 2.92%, which is an A- average credit rating.

Best of all, the valuations of the three stocks are appealing, with the trio trading at an average discount to our fair value estimates of 16%. Together, they have an average 19% upside through June 2026 and an average annual total return potential of 13% by the end of 2030.

data.title: Two Quality Stocks Whose Price Declines May Be Long-Term Buying Opportunities
data.description:
Introduction

Typically, when stock prices pull back without any obvious reason, investors often wonder if there's something wrong with the business. Maybe the market is pricing in something we don't see.

Mr. Market is often forward-looking, so price declines before any news is very reasonable. However, in the case of these two companies, I think their price declines could be potential buying opportunities if you're a long-term investor.

In this article, I discuss two stocks and why investors should consider taking advantage of their recent price pullbacks.

Be Greedy When Others Are Fearful

Most of us are likely familiar with this quote from the Oracle of Omaha, Warren Buffett. But every dip may not be a great buying opportunity. Although when it comes to fundamentally sound businesses, it's prudent to take advantage of potential opportunities.

Sometimes the dip can keep on dipping, especially when buying cyclical companies that are experiencing financial difficulties. One example of this is Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA).

I bought the company for around $36 a share as I thought this was a great buying opportunity. Unfortunately, the stock price kept declining as their turnaround efforts weren't having any merit.

But what prompted me to sell my shares and downgrade them from a hold to a sell back in October of 2023 was their failure to raise the dividend, something they had done for years. The stock was not far from joining the prestigious list of Dividend Kings.

After not raising the dividend and giving a vague answer regarding the dividend policy, I figured a dividend cut was inevitable. Some readers argued with me that I was overthinking it and that the dividend was safe. Three months later, the operator cut its dividend by nearly 48%.

Over the past decade, WBA is down almost 88% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), up roughly 193% over the same period. Walgreens has performed so poorly that it has agreed to a deal with Sycamore Partners to be taken private.

While the two companies I discuss today have been in the green over the long term, their prices have retreated in recent months.

Without further ado, let's get into these two potential opportunities.

#1 T-Mobile US (TMUS): Everything Verizon Should Be

In the chart below, you can see T-Mobile's price has pulled back from its 52-week high of over $276 a share. At the time of writing, the stock is down another $4 a share to $228.

And while some may say the stock was overvalued to begin with, I think many are confusing it with the typical telecom stock. A slow grower, high debt load plodder who typically sees 1%–2% growth a year. Basically Verizon (VZ).

I have nothing against Verizon, as I currently own shares and think they deserve a spot in every income-focused investor's portfolio. But how T-Mobile has been operating lately—they're everything I think Verizon should be.

Verizon was often known for its quality network. But they have been losing market share to T-Mobile. T-Mobile has not only been making acquisitions to grow the business but also raising its dividend while buying back shares.

Something TMUS did well was wait to start paying dividends so they could focus on the business. Verizon has been paying dividends for close to 20 years, while T-Mobile declared its first in 2023.

Since then, the telecom giant has conducted one increase, an impressive 35.4% (increase) last September. And for its upcoming increase later this year, I expect another impressive double-digit one, although likely not as large as last year.

They also announced a $50 billion buyback program at the same time, something I've criticized Verizon for doing in past articles. Through 2029, analysts expect double-digit earnings growth from TMUS. As a result, the stock is likely to see a strong upside, as stock prices typically follow earnings growth.

In its latest quarter, the telecom giant showed impressive double-digit growth year-over-year. EPS beat analysts' estimates, amounting to $2.58, up from $2.00 in the previous year's quarter.

Within its segments, they also saw double-digit growth, with the exception of Postpaid Phone Net Customers and Postpaid Net Accounts. Both declined from 523k and 218k, respectively, likely a result of higher for longer interest rates.

But both net income and free cash flow grew 24% and 31% from the prior year. Impressive considering the challenging economic backdrop. Adjusted EBITDA showed a solid 8% increase, allowing management to raise its EBITDA outlook to $33.2 billion–$33.7 billion for 2025.

But the main metric is that free cash flow is expected to grow this year as well. 2024's free cash flow amounted to $17 billion, and for 2025, management expects this in a range of $17.5 billion to $18 billion. For comparison, Verizon expects 2025's free cash flow to be between $17.5 billion and $18.5 billion, down from $19.8 billion in 2024.

As you can see, T-Mobile's free cash flow sufficiently covered its dividend, giving the company ample room for double-digit increases and share repurchases in the years ahead. In 2024, management repurchased approximately $11.1 billion worth of shares.

And these will also play a part in strong earnings growth. They've also been focusing on deleveraging the balance sheet with a net debt to adjusted EBITDA ratio of 2.4x, down from 2.5x the previous year.

The company is expected to report Q2 earnings at the end of this month, and analysts expect earnings per share of $2.68, a $0.10 increase from Q1. I expect TMUS to deliver another beat with EPS in a range of $2.66 to $2.80 a share.

Recently, Bank of America (BAC) reinstated coverage on T-Mobile with a price target of $255, an 11.84% increase from the current price.

However, by the end of next year, I expect its stock price could be well over $300 a share if we manage to dodge a recession or market crash. So, if you're a long-term investor, T-Mobile's price pullback may be a good buying opportunity.

#2 Agree Realty (ADC): The Net Lease Costco

Agree Realty is another stock that has pulled back from a recent 52-week high. The REIT traded near $80 a share before pulling back to $71, where it currently trades. Moreover, with interest rates likely to be lower in the next year, Agree Realty's share price is likely to see upside as a result.

Although Agree Realty doesn't see the high growth of Costco (COST), its management team and execution remind me of a net lease Costco.

Particularly, ADC is attractive because of its long growth runway. At the end of Q1, the REIT had 2,977 properties, all based in the U.S.

So, they haven't even scratched the surface yet in terms of growth. Investments in Europe, the U.K., and other countries remain an option going forward.

But for now, I expect Agree Realty to continue executing on acquisitions in the continental United States. One indicator of its strong fundamentals and forward-thinking management team is its raised investment guidance for 2025. Management now expects acquisitions in between $1.3 billion and $1.5 billion for the year.

They also increased its AFFO guidance a penny to $4.27–$4.30, representing 3.5% growth from the prior year. One key advantage ADC has over peers, even big brother Realty Income (O), despite O's higher credit rating, is its minimal debt maturities and high exposure to investment-rated tenants.

Besides minimal debt maturing until 2028, ADC has been raising capital and has over $2 billion in liquidity in Q1. With virtually no debt for 3 years, this gives them plenty of flexibility to make acquisitions to continue growing the portfolio.

Leverage was also very conservative, with a net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of 3.3x. And I expect management will operate within the 3.0x–4.0x range.

Going forward, I wouldn't be surprised to see them receive another upgrade to its credit rating in the next 2 to 3 years, which would also positively impact the share price as this would give them cost of capital advantages.

The most obvious reason Agree Realty should be considered is its monthly dividend. Last quarter, its AFFO payout ratio was 72%.

I typically look for quality REITs to operate in the 70%–75% range. Anything below 70% is even better. Over the past 10 years, Agree Realty's dividend has been well covered by its cash flows.

And I don't expect this to change anytime soon. AFFO grew to $1.06 while core FFO grew $0.02 to $1.04. The REIT is also reporting earnings at the end of this month, with analysts anticipating core FFO to retract a penny. I expect core FFO in a range of $1.04–$1.07 and AFFO between $1.05–$1.08 a share.

ADC also offers solid upside over the next two years. Furthermore, if interest rates are significantly lower than where they are now, I expect Agree Realty's price could move much higher, likely in the $80–$85 range at least. So, the pullback closer to $70–$71 a share may be as good as it gets.

Risks To Thesis

Both T-Mobile and Agree Realty could be impacted by a slowing economy. The telecom giant would likely see slowing net adds in its segments, while ADC could see impacts to its occupancy and slower investment activity.

Although recession fears seem to have dissipated in recent months, I don't think one is out of the question in the next 6 to 12 months. But as a result of its fundamentals, any further price drops should be viewed as opportunities.

Conclusion

Although both stocks still may seem relatively expensive, its strong fundamentals and execution in a challenging economic backdrop show its quality and that they warrant premiums in comparison to peers.

Moreover, both stand to see tailwinds from lower interest rates, which could also drive significant price appreciation in the next 6 to 12 months.

As a result, investors should view T-Mobile and Agree Realty's price pullbacks as potential long-term buying opportunities.

data.title: Wall Street Breakfast Podcast: Circle K Owner Ends 7-Eleven Pursuit
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) pulls $47B takeover bid for 7-11 parent (OTCPK:SVNDY). (00:22) PBS, NPR face funding loss as Senate approves DOGE-backed budget cuts. (01:11) Wix.com (WIX) climbs after Barclays reveals hike on subscription plans. (02:11)

This is an abridged transcript.

Alimentation Couche-Tard has withdrawn its $47B proposal to buy Seven & i Holdings.

Canada’s convenience store giant (OTCPK:ANCTF) said Wednesday (OTCPK:SVNDY) (OTCPK:SVNDF) that the 7-Eleven parent company had a "persistent lack of good faith engagement."

The surprise move marks the end of a year-long attempt by Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) who operates Circle K to dramatically expand its convenience store empire.

Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) said it repeatedly sought a friendly dialogue with Seven & i's (OTCPK:SVNDY) (OTCPK:SVNDF) founding family but that the Japanese company was not open to any conversation about the takeover proposal.

Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF) raised its offer to $47B in October 2024 from $38.5B, and in March offered to increase it further if Seven & i (OTCPK:SVNDY) (OTCPK:SVNDF) cooperated and revealed more financial information.

Republicans are poised to achieve their long-standing goal of eliminating federal funding for public broadcasting.

The Senate approved a $9 billion package of cuts tied to the Department of Government Efficiency initiative.

The measure passed in a 51-48 vote, targeting the Public Broadcasting Service, National Public Radio, and various foreign aid programs.

Two of the Senate’s 53 Republicans, Lisa Murkowski of Alaska and Susan Collins of Maine, joined Democrats in voting against the legislation.

The bulk of the funds targeted — about $8 billion — was for foreign assistance programs. The remaining $1.1 billion was for the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, which funds PBS and NPR. The House is expected to give final approval to the package this week before sending it to President Trump for his signature.

According to the Corporation for Public Broadcasting, Congress has fully funded it through Sept 30, 2027.

Wix.com (NASDAQ:WIX) finished the trading day on Wednesday 4.2% higher.

Barclays revealed that the website development platform had increased its subscription plans.

Barclays' price tracker identified increases on Wix's two and three-year plans ranging from 8 to 21%.

Barclays maintains its Overweight rating and $240 price target on Wix.com.

What’s Trending on Seeking Alpha:

Is Mexican Coke coming to America?

AI bubble bigger than dot-com? Apollo economist sounds the alarm

Taiwan Semi rises after Q2 profit surges 60%, raises 2025 outlook

Catalyst watch:

  • It is the 70th anniversary of the Disneyland Resort in Anaheim, California (DIS). Disneyland is widely regarded as one of the most important and influential theme parks in history.

  • Merck (MRK) will host an investor event where scientific and commercial leaders will give an overview of the company’s advancing research pipeline in HIV treatment and prevention.

Stocks came off a volatile session on Wednesday, especially after reports of Trump's plans for Powell, but eventually ended in the green. Major averages also benefited from a softer-than-anticipated wholesale inflation report.

Trump said on Wednesday that it's “highly unlikely” he'll fire Powell “unless he has to leave for fraud.” Earlier reporting on the day indicated that the President would fire the Fed Chair soon.

Dow, S&P and Nasdaq futures are in mixed territory. Crude oil is flat at $66/barrel. Bitcoin is up 0.3% at $119,000. Gold is down 0.5% at $3,328.

The FTSE 100 is up 0.5% and the DAX is up 0.9%.

The biggest movers for the day premarket: Sarepta Therapeutics (NASDAQ:SRPT) +31% — Shares surged after the company announced a strategic review that includes a 36% workforce reduction (~500 jobs) and a sharp refocus of its pipeline.

On today’s economic calendar:

  • 8:30 am Retail Sales

  • 10:00 am Business Inventories

  • 12:45 pm San Francisco Federal Reserve Bank President Mary Daly will participate in a moderated conversation before the 2025 Rocky Mounty Economic Summit.

  • 6:30 pm Federal Reserve Board Governor Christopher Waller will speak on the economic outlook before the Money Marketeers of New York University.

data.title: Missed The Circle Rally? Crypto Miner BitFuFu Might Offer The Next Big Opportunity
data.description:
If you feel like you've missed the hype around stablecoins, especially with all the attention on Circle (CRCL) (feel free to check out my previous story on Circle, where I laid out the potential and risks of the company), I will suggest looking at crypto mining companies. Personally, I am very optimistic about their future, and here's why.

I began investing in Bitcoin (BTC-USD) about five years ago, when the price was already around $20K per coin. Back then, I couldn't even afford a full Bitcoin. Since then, the price soared to $60K, dropped back to $20K, but look at the price right now. BTC just hit an all-time high at $123K on July 14, with people expecting the upcoming Crypto Week to bring new regulatory updates and major positive developments.

Despite macroeconomic uncertainty, trade tensions, and the volatile cycles of altcoins, I remain a firm believer in BTC and Ethereum (ETH-USD). 95% of BTC has been mined, only 5% left. Hence, the supply is limited, but the demand is growing dramatically. Bitcoin is gaining broader acceptance, not only among crypto enthusiasts like myself but also from major financial institutions.

Bloomberg reported that "Citadel is looking to become a liquidity provider for cryptocurrencies, betting President Donald Trump's embrace of the industry will usher in a boom for the asset class." More recent news showed that institutions like ETFs, big companies, and asset managers are actively buying BTC to diversify their investment portfolios. CNBC says, "Public companies acquired about 131,000 coins in the second quarter, growing their bitcoin balance 18%, according to data provider Bitcoin Treasuries. ETFs showed an 8% increase, or about 111,000 BTC, in the same period." Tom Lee projected that BTC can go above 150k by the end of the year, and the current price is not that far from this ambitious goal.

When the BTC price goes up, miners benefit. All miners hold large amounts of BTC themselves, so crypto mining stocks show a strong correlation to the underlying Bitcoin price. But BTC price is not the only factor/driver for mining companies' valuation. The two main metrics to evaluate crypto mining companies are hashrate (computational power) and power capacity.

A higher hashrate basically means the company has a better shot at winning new Bitcoin blocks, and more blocks mean more revenue. The way to get a higher hashrate is by upgrading to better and more efficient mining machines. For example, upgrading from an Antminer S9 to an S19 Pro could more than double the hashrate per machine. At the same time, increasing power capacity shows you the company has the infrastructure to keep scaling. That's huge, especially with energy costs climbing. Without that kind of backbone, it's tough to stay profitable long term.

Valuation Comparison

After checking out some of the major mining companies' recent operation reports, I summarized the comparison above, I feel BitFuFu (NASDAQ:FUFU) is significantly undervalued. I got all hashrate numbers from their respective June operation reports. This table shows that BitFuFu already has a higher hashrate than Riot (RIOT), and its power capacity is rapidly growing, soon approaching 1 GW. Yet, their market cap is only a fraction of Riot's, 1/7 to be more exact. Of course, BTC holding is the largest differentiator. Even considering Riot's larger Bitcoin holdings, this valuation gap still seems disproportionately large. If I subtract Bitcoin holdings from the market cap to focus purely on operational value comparison, Riot still stands around $2.2B in market cap compared to BitFuFu's approximately $377M.

To me, this gap in valuation makes BitFuFu a compelling investment opportunity that deserves attention. Here's my take on the valuation: If I were to value BitFuFu similarly to Riot based on their hash rate and operational scale, its market cap would be substantially higher.

As I calculated before, Riot's operational business (excluding Bitcoin holdings) is valued at roughly $2.2B, which means investors value each unit of Riot's mining power (each EH/s) at approximately $2.2B/35.5 EH/s = $62M/EH/s.

In terms of the cost of power, Riot's past operation reports listed $0.026/kWh to $0.03/kWh in different months, which is very similar to BitFuFu's $0.03/kWh. Normalizing for these energy costs and Riot's slightly lower expense cuts the premium multiple by roughly 10%. Applying the 10% reduction to the $62M/EH/s multiple yields an implied multiple of around $55.8M/EH/s, translating to an operational value of approximately $2.02B for BitFuFu.

After adding BitFuFu's existing Bitcoin holdings, the total valuation should reasonably reach about $2.24B. With BitFuFu's current market cap around $600M, this analysis suggests its share price could rise significantly to about $14 per share.

Of course, given that BitFuFu is headquartered in Singapore (unlike Riot, which is U.S.-based), is less widely recognized than industry leaders like Riot and Marathon (MARA), and has under half Riot's power capacity, I apply a 50% risk discount to its valuation. Therefore, a more conservative estimate for BitFuFu is about $1.01 billion, implying a share price near $7, still well above its current market level.

This quick, high-level valuation aligns with H.C. Wainwright's research report. According to the report, H.C. Wainwright issued a "Buy" rating and a $7 price target for BitFuFu. The analyst used a 3x market cap to sales ratio on a $383.5 million 2025 sales estimate for the $7 price target.

Cloud Mining Shifts Price Risk to Clients

One big differentiator between BitFuFu and Riot is that Riot is primarily a self-mining operation, as is MARA. But BitFuFu has a big cloud mining business. I got to know about BitFuFu because I was looking for cloud mining providers to expand my BTC inventory. According to its June report, the company mined "445 BTC in June 2025, an 11.3% month-over-month increase, including an 8.4% increase from cloud mining to 387 BTC and a 34.9% increase from self-mining to 58 BTC. Cloud Mining Users: 623,114 registered users as of June 30, 2025."

This shows the company's cloud mining business is scaling rapidly, and demand is huge, which aligns with what we discussed above: more company and institutional buyers. Unlike self-mining, cloud mining is a less risky business, because clients pay a fixed percentage of fees in exchange for mining returns, which helps stabilize BitFuFu's revenue even in volatile markets.

Backed by Bitmain

Another big reason I believe in this stock is that the company is backed by Bitmain. According to BitFuFu's prospectus, Bitmain is the founding investor and partner of BitFuFu. In fact, the founder of BitFuFU is a former Bitmain business director. Just to give some context, Bitmain is the dominant player in the Bitcoin mining hardware market, and MARA and RIOT use Bitmain machines too.

Because of the tight relationship, BitFuFU could have early access to the latest and most cost-efficient mining equipment. Newer mining rigs like Bitmain's latest Antminers typically consume less electricity per hash (measured as W/TH), significantly reducing operational costs.

Conclusion

There's no denying it anymore: BTC is entering the mainstream. In fact, research has shown that Bitcoin volatility is now smaller than the S&P 500 and Nasdaq indices. With strong backing from big financial institutions and President Trump, the tide is clearly turning in favor of digital assets. If you think the Bitcoin price is too high, definitely consider crypto mining stocks like BitFuFu. While the recent Bitcoin halving has squeezed miners a bit, slashing block rewards always does. This is not new. It's part of the natural rhythm of the industry. The players that will come out ahead are the ones that are built to last. BitFuFu, for example, has strong cash flow and is expanding its power capacity, backed by the largest mining equipment provider, Bitmain, putting it in a solid position to ride this out and come out stronger.

If BitFuFu can actually hit its ambitious goal of hitting 1 GW of mining capacity by the end of 2026, there's real potential for its valuation to jump. But of course, this is still a small-cap stock, which means higher risk. Volatility is part of the package. But the upside can be huge if things play out well.

data.title: Pricing, Tariffs, Social Inflation To Top U.S. P&C Earnings Agendas
data.description:
A changing pricing environment and renewed social inflation worries will be among the focal points for US property and casualty insurers when they release second quarter results.

Executives are expected to talk about the pace of pricing increases slowing after enjoying a historic year of underwriting profitability in 2024. Both the personal auto and homeowners segments booked year-over-year double-digit increases in direct premiums written last year as combined ratios eased.

Pricing and demand trends against the backdrop of tariff-driven inflationary pressures will be elements of the "big picture" for earnings calls, according to CFRA Research analyst Cathy Seifert.

"I think after a strong 2024, a lot of the tailwinds have gone," Seifert said in an interview. "We've got some headwinds and uncertainty, but we've also got a stable to acceptable pricing environment. We just don't have a whole lot of really strong tailwinds right now coming off a strong 2024."

An "overarching" issue is how tariffs and the possible "mass deportation" of migrants in the US impact the demand for insurance as well as "input costs, [such as] lumber, construction and auto parts," she added.

Earnings season kicks off in the morning of July 17 with The Travelers Cos. Inc. (TRV).

Ratios recover

An S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis found that sell-side analysts expect combined ratios to improve both sequentially and year-over-year for a majority of the 16 largest publicly traded US property and casualty and multiline insurers. Analysts predict that 10 companies will show improvement from the first quarter, which was overshadowed by the January wildfires in the Los Angeles area, while 11 are expected to show year-over-year improvement.

Four companies are expected to book ratios below 90%: Kinsale Capital Group Inc. (KNSL), Chubb (CB), AXIS Capital Holdings Ltd. (AXS), and The Progressive Corp. (PGR).

Assurant Inc. (AIZ) and Cincinnati Financial Corp. (CINF) are the only companies expected to post ratios above 100%, which would indicate underwriting losses. Assurant is the lone company in this analysis expected to see deterioration in its ratio both sequentially and year-over-year.

Catastrophe losses for the quarter appear to be "normal" based on April and May results published by Progressive and The Allstate Corp. (ALL), according to Piper Sandler analyst Paul Newsome. Progressive reported catastrophe losses of $206 million in April and $275 million in May, while Allstate reported $594 million and $777 million in losses, respectively.

Earnings bounce back

Sell-side analysts expect EPS to increase sequentially for 12 of the 17 companies in the analysis, while 13 will report higher earnings compared to the second quarter of 2024.

Companies expected to show both sequential and year-over-year improvements in EPS include Chubb, Travelers, Allstate, AIG (AIG), The Hartford Insurance Group Inc. (HIG), Kinsale Capital, Markel Group Inc. (MKL), Cincinnati Financial and Assurant. RLI Corp. (RLI) is the lone carrier expected to report annual and sequential decreases in EPS.

Progressive's revenues jump

Sell-siders anticipate revenues improving, with 14 of 17 expected to see higher revenues compared to the last quarter and 16 of 17 projected to see increases over the prior-year quarter.

Progressive should top the analysis in total revenues for the ninth straight quarter, with a consensus estimate of $21.70 billion. That would be a 19.70% year-over-year increase and a 6.32% improvement a quarter earlier.

Arch Capital Group Ltd. (ACGL) and Allstate are expected to report sequential decreases in revenue, while The Hartford is set to log decreases both sequentially and year-over-year.

Litigation worries back on radar

Social inflation, a topic that had faded into the background in the aftermath of the COVID-19 pandemic, is front and center once again as litigation that was delayed by pandemic-related court closings is making its way back to the judicial system.

Chubb Ltd. CEO Evan Greenberg, one of the most vocal executives on the topic, sounded the alarm once again in a July 7 op-ed he co-wrote with John Doyle, CEO of Marsh McLennan Cos. Inc., for The Wall Street Journal, referring to litigation costs as "a hidden tax."

"The volume of litigation is growing rapidly," Greenberg and Doyle wrote. "Cases are becoming more expensive to resolve, and people and businesses everywhere are footing the bill. As the leaders of Chubb and Marsh McLennan, we see the real costs of this hidden tax every day through our customers."

Piper Sandler's Newsome said social inflation makes it imperative insurers keep a close eye on their casualty reserves.

"Companies differ greatly in their view of which products are most affected, which geographies are the most affected, the actual rate of social inflation, and the accident years most affected," Newsome wrote in a note to clients. "This suggests to us that, other than raising rates, the insurers have no long-term fix for the problem."

Investors shrugging off tariffs

Equity investors are moving away from defensive characteristics provided by P&C carriers' stocks in favor of those that are "more economically sensitive," according to UBS Global Research analyst Brian Meredith. The S&P Composite 1500 Property & Casualty Insurance Index, which fell 3.35% during the second quarter, has fallen an additional 4% since the start of the third quarter.

One of the reasons for the underperformance is that tariffs do not appear to be as much of a threat as they did at first, said Keefe, Bruyette and Woods analyst Meyer Shields.

"Everything melted down when it looked like tariffs would disrupt the economy," Shields said in an interview. "Broader investors have said, 'We don't think tariffs are going to be that disruptive,' and I think that's why a lot of things have recovered and the defensive appeal of property casualty insurance has faded in that context."

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Staying The Course: Navigating A Volatile Q2 In Commodities
data.description:
Commodities remained resilient in Q2 2025; oil was volatile, gold rose, metals fell. Resource equities were mixed but showed strong fundamentals despite macro uncertainty.

Q2 2025 Commodities & Resource Equities: Resilience Through Uncertainty

Commodities and resource equities remained resilient in Q2 2025, navigating a complex macroeconomic and geopolitical backdrop. Oil markets experienced pronounced volatility, driven by evolving OPEC+ supply dynamics and heightened geopolitical risk. Gold outperformed, reaffirming its status as a safe-haven asset amid global uncertainty. Industrial metals, however, came under pressure due to softer demand trends—particularly from China’s industrial sector.

Resource equities delivered a mixed performance, though the broader sector continued to reflect key investment strengths: robust operational execution, disciplined capital allocation, and an ongoing commitment to shareholder returns. While investor sentiment remained cautious—shaped by persistent macro and policy uncertainty—the underlying fundamentals and long-term value drivers in the space remain intact.

Sector Performance Recap

  • Oil & Gas – Oil markets experienced significant volatility in Q2. Crude prices fell to four-year lows in early May but rebounded sharply following the strikes against Iran’s nuclear infrastructure. These gains were short-lived, however, as the subsequent de-escalation redirected market focus to weakening global demand and OPEC+’s gradual rollback of voluntary production cuts. U.S. shale producers maintained a conservative approach to capital expenditures amid margin compression and elevated input costs driven by new tariffs. In contrast, U.S. refiners outperformed thanks to robust shareholder return strategies and firm capital discipline.
  • Base & Industrial Metals – Base metal prices delivered mixed results. Copper traded near multi-month highs, supported by ongoing supply constraints and relatively stable demand from China. U.S. exchange inventories fell to their lowest levels in nearly two years, driven by pre-tariff stockpiling and persistent mining disruptions. Supply remains tight due to project delays at major developments, while Chinese smelters face margin compression from historically low treatment and refining charges. In the iron ore market, resilient steel production and declining Chinese port inventories offered bullish signals, but broader macroeconomic concerns—including trade and property sector instability—kept prices under pressure.
  • Gold & Precious Metals – Gold prices remained robust, briefly dipping below $3,000/oz in April amid a risk-on rally before swiftly recovering. Gold equities generally outperformed the metal, supported by stable all-in sustaining costs (AISC) and strong free cash flow generation. Producers capitalized on favorable price conditions to strengthen balance sheets—reduce debt, enhance dividend payouts, and pursue strategic M&A activity. Recent consolidation trends have created opportunities for operational synergies and reserve replacement.
  • Renewables & Alternatives – The renewable energy sector continued to demonstrate modest growth in Q2 2025, though emerging policy and macroeconomic headwinds have raised new uncertainties. China remained the global leader, installing a record 60 GW of solar capacity, while the U.S. added 10 GW of battery storage in Q1 alone. However, proposed U.S. federal policy changes—such as potential reductions to production and investment tax credits, and the introduction of excise taxes on solar and wind—pose risks to project costs and development pipelines. Elevated interest rates and capital costs continue to challenge equipment manufacturers and independent power producers.
  • Agriculture/Paper & Forest Products – The agricultural sector presented a mixed outlook. Grain markets remained under pressure, with corn and soybean prices falling below breakeven levels for many U.S. farmers, despite generally favorable growing conditions. Margins continued to be compressed by rising input costs and ongoing trade policy uncertainty. Fertilizer prices surged, driven by supply disruption in the Middle East. Conversely, protein markets showed relative strength as tight supplies of cattle, hogs and chickens supported higher prices across beef, pork and poultry. Nonetheless, packaged food producers faced pressure from elevated production costs, tariff-induced input inflation, and subdued consumer demand.

Portfolio Performance: Drivers and Detractors

The Global Resources Fund (Class A; excluding fees and expenses, the “Fund”)(GHAAX) returned 5.47% in the second quarter of 2025, outperforming its benchmark, the S&P Global Natural Resources Index (the “Index”), which returned 3.26%. Year-to-date, the Fund has returned 13.34%, also ahead of the Index’s 10.42% return over the same period.

On an absolute basis, the top contributors to Fund performance were positions in Base & Industrial Metals—notably copper producers and diversified miners—as well as Gold & Precious Metals producers. Fertilizer companies within Agriculture also added to returns. Detractors included Oil & Gas and Paper & Forest positions, with integrated oil and gas producers accounting for the largest declines.

Relative to the Index, the Fund benefited from strong security selection within Base & Industrial Metals, an underweight allocation and effective selection in Oil & Gas, and an overweight in Renewables & Alternatives. However, performance was negatively impacted by selection and interaction effects in Agriculture.

From Policy Shifts to Price Signals: Key Market Drivers and Sector Impacts for the Second Half

The U.S. dollar is expected to remain under pressure, with downside driven by renewed tariff threats and growing concerns over fiscal sustainability. The passage of Trump’s "Big Beautiful Bill" could further widen budget deficits. Historically, a weaker dollar has supported higher commodity prices—a dynamic we expect to persist into the second half of 2025.

Geopolitical risk remains elevated, particularly in the Middle East. Although a ceasefire with Iran is currently in place, its long-term viability is uncertain. We believe a 5-10% risk premium is currently warranted for oil prices until a lasting resolution is achieved or market fundamentals regain control. Importantly, the distinction between oil at $60–$65 versus $55 is critical for high-cost producers and exploration-intensive firms. The current environment favors well-capitalized oil and gas companies with low break-even points, healthy balance sheets, robust profitability, and attractive shareholder return profiles via dividends and buybacks.

In the metals space, companies are benefiting from improved operational efficiency and resilient commodity prices. Meanwhile, volatility continues to plague the renewables sector as clarity around tax credits, other fiscal incentives and enforcement rules remain unclear. In agriculture, low crop prices should bolster the margins of protein players who will benefit from subdued animal feed costs. Conversely, subdued crop prices present challenges for fertilizers and agrochemical producers. Finally, the macroeconomic environment remains unfavorable for paper and forest products companies, where demand softness and cost pressures continue to weigh on performance.
