data.title: Stitch Fix: Like But Don't Love Turnaround Story
data.description:
I reviewed Stitch Fix (SFIX) earlier this year and gave the company a “Hold” rating at that time. Although the company’s new CEO was making progress with his new initiatives, revenue continued to decline as the company’s customer base shrunk.

Since that article, the company’s stock has declined by roughly 5% while the S&P is up by over 5%. YTD Stitch Fix is flat.

While Stitch Fix has continued to make headway towards growing the business once again, not enough progress has been made for me to turn bullish on the company.

Let’s dig into the company’s latest quarter and see how Stitch Fix plans to continue reigniting growth.

With The Foundation Built, Growth Comes Next

In my last article, I noted Stitch Fix’s three-phase strategy, which was first rationalize then build and the last phase is growth. I would say the company has executed on the first two phases and is now entering phase three.

CEO Matt Baer stated the same on the earnings call, noting the considerable progress the company has made, “Over the last 2 years, we have worked through the first 2 phases of our strategy, rationalize and build, and fundamentally reshaped how we operate. We've strengthened the foundation of our business, incorporating retail best practices in strategic areas such as pricing, warehouse optimization and inventory management that enable us to operate more efficiently. We've also brought to life a more modern and dynamic Stitch Fix through our refreshed brand identity and the progress we have made reimagining our client experience. The improvements to our client experience includes 4 areas of focus: enhancing client engagement features, deepening client-stylists relationships, introducing increased flexibility to our service and offering a stronger assortment with more fresh and new styles.”

There are a few key strategies which I believe have led to the company finally returning to revenue growth. One of those strategies was letting customers have up to eight items in their fix. This tactic allows for more flexibility and gives the consumer more options which in turn could lead to a larger order for Stitch Fix. I believe it’s also giving the company’s internal stylists as well as their AI tools more data, as these insights can give the company additional information to help improve future fixes.

On the earnings call, management noted the company will begin letting first-time consumers have an eight-item fix so Stitch Fix can begin to accumulate more data for the user right from the first order. Additionally, the company is testing theme fixes, some examples of which include summer and workout fixes. Personally, I think those are great ideas as some customers may be looking to add variety to their wardrobe for summer vacations or when beginning to train for a new endeavor such as marathon or 10K.

Another new addition to Stitch Fix that has increased revenue is the addition of adjacent categories like shoes and jewelry. These new products seem to be successful thus far, especially footwear which has seen 35% growth year-over-year.

Overall, I think there is a lot to like with Stitch Fix’s offering and it’s creating a unique and unmatched value proposition within the apparel market. Consumers can quickly have clothes selected for them with minimal effort required, within their budget and with enough items to hopefully get their desired results.

Currently, it seems that consumer spending is holding steady as research from Deloitte suggests spending intentions remain consistent with 72% of respondents stating they’ve made at least one “splurge” purchase in May. Of these splurge purchases, coming in second is purchases related to clothing and accessories as you can see below:

With consumer spending holding steady, and Stitch Fix continuing to gain market share according to insights from Circana, the company seems to be on a path to steadily reignite growth.

AI is a Double-Edged Sword

I think one of the key reasons for Stitch Fix’s turnaround aside from the personalized stylists, flexible fixes and new product offerings, is the company’s implementation of artificial intelligence. Not only does it help Stitch Fix get insights for a specific consumer, but the company’s AI capabilities help the business with their overall merchandising strategy by making many of the buying decisions.

I imagine other retailers are likely using AI to help with similar buying decisions; however, I don’t think many are using AI to successfully shop for one specific consumer, or at least, not to the same degree as Stitch Fix. However, with reward programs and consumer data I could see more and more companies implementing personalized recommendations for consumers. For instance, if a runner continues to purchase the same socks and shoes from a specific company, I could easily see a customized email in which the company asks the consumer if another purchase is needed based on client history.

Now, I do think Stitch Fix has an edge considering they have a first mover advantage and thus many consumers look to their brand if they are looking to purchase something new or perhaps something out of his or her comfort zone. Yet, AI makes Stitch Fix's moat far more penetrable and the company with better algorithms and thus the better recommendations will likely win repeat customers.

Financials

In Q3 2025, Stitch Fix generated revenue of $325 million which is a slight increase (less than 1%) compared to Q3 2024. After numerous quarters in which revenue continued to decline compared to the prior year, it’s certainly positive that the company finally saw an increase as you can see below:

Stitch Fix now estimates they will earn between $298 to $303 million in the fourth quarter. The company has raised their full-year guidance as they now expect revenues of $1.254 to $1.259 billion for the full 2025 fiscal year. However, despite this increase, the company still expects revenues will be down year-over-year.

At the end of the Q3 2025, the company had 2,353,000 active clients which is a decrease of less than 1% quarter-over-quarter and a 10% decline compared to the prior year. Stitch Fix continues to lose active clients quarter after quarter as the graphic illustrates below:

However, on a positive note, revenue per active client is up and this figure has continued to rise over the last few quarters so the company is at least getting their loyal customers to spend more:

Compared to fiscal year 2024, the company has significantly reduced their selling, general, and administrative expenses, which has led to smaller operating losses and net losses as you can see for the three and nine month periods shown below:

The company’s gross margins were 44.2% for the quarter which is worse compared to the last few quarters as you can see below:

Management noted for the quarter gross profit margins fell due to lower product margins as the company sought more of variety of inventory.

Stitch Fix has maintained an excellent balance sheet with no long-term debt and enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities as you can see below:

Valuation

Stitch Fix has a valuation grade of a “A-” at Seeking Alpha:

Given Stitch Fix remains unprofitable, I think price-to-sales is the best metric valuation for this business. Stitch Fix does have a forward price-to-sales ratio which is better than the sector median and compared to peer The RealReal (REAL).

Compared to my prior article, Stitch Fix's forward price to sales ratio has come down compared earlier in the year as you can see below:

Despite Stitch Fix seeming more attractive from a forward price-to-sales perspective, future revenue growth doesn't appear promising over the next couple of years:

Comparability, the RealReal is expecting to see double-digit revenue growth by analysts, which is likely why Wall Street is more bullish on the RealReal compared to Stitch Fix.

Conclusion

Stitch Fix’s turnaround story continues as the company has finally generated positive revenue growth illustrating the company’s strategies which include, flexible fixes and continued variety, seem to be working.

Yet, the company continues to lose clients and remains unprofitable despite significant reductions in expenses. Also, while the company is using AI to improve the business, I could easily see a competitor mirroring Stitch Fix’s customized approach.

While the valuation seems attractive, the company’s outlook seems far from stellar. Until the company can make consistent progress in getting new clients and getting these clients to continue to spend more per fix, I remain on the sidelines.

data.title: EFA: A Flawed Hedge With Diminishing Long-Term Appeal
data.description:
The iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA) appears as bland as it can get. One of the most popular international ETFs with an AUM of over $63b, EFA fails to impress by recent performance or hedging advantages. It tracks the MSCI EAFE index betting on developed markets ex-US and ex-Canada. Both the countries and stocks are selected on broadly a backward looking market cap criteria and currency effects are fully passed through.

Capturing Growth In The Developed World

As it turns out, the promise of growth outside the US from the developed world has not worked out in the past decade and more for long-term investors. However, the hedging and outperformance virtues become clearer when one looks at the last decade as an example. The MSCI EAFE index did outperform the S&P 500 for large sustained periods of time during the decade.

Hedging And Diversification

Directional hedging is still a drag i.e. don't expect EFA to bail your portfolio out when the US markets are crashing. Not at least for sustained large periods of time. The correlation has always reverted. The hedging benefits therefore be viewed from a outperformance lens alone. There could be periods where the US markets stagnate and EFA could be reasonably positive. Although equally possible is an underperformance from EFA. It is evident therefore that EFA is not a blind hedge. It may have diversification benefits over a very long term (spreading risks), but no sustained hedging benefit.

Diversification Continued

With most of the allocation concentrated in Europe and Japan, diversification is not truly met in the international context. Not as long as emerging markets are missing. Emerging markets do bring in added risks of political and policy uncertainties, but I am now talking from a risk diversification perspective alone.

In terms of growth prospects too, Japan is faced with an aging demographics and Europe with lower number of innovation/disruption focused mega caps that the US offers. Structural hurdles make the growth case no better than the US - as reflected in the past decade's returns.

Many US stocks now have sufficient global exposure anyway to ride diversified risks and growth stories - from Microsoft to Coca Cola. EFA with its conservative tilt towards low growth sectors like financials, still appears bland in comparison - all for international exposure that could be redundant. So yes, theoretical diversification is met in EFA, but it feels part growth stuck and part redundant - particularly in today's era.

Concentration risks are well handled and drawdowns could be more limited though. Within the EFA portfolio, the top 10 holdings contribute to only 12.75% of the overall portfolio. The portfolio is a large collection of 700+ stocks as well. So a few names crashing does not impact returns and idiosyncratic risks are extremely low.

Currency Impacts

The additional layer of currency gains if the dollar weakens is not a material impact in my opinion. For those who want to hedge the dollar against trade and fiscal deficits or easing rates, the impact on EFA is unlikely to cross single digit favorable alpha. A far lower worry to hedge against compared to equity risk and return profiles.

Yields Are Comforting But Alternatives Exist

For passive income oriented investors though EFA could make sense. A stable 2.5% -3% yield on an underlying with lower drawdown risks looks decent. However, drawdowns, although lower than say QQQ, are significant enough. In the 2022-23 rate hike cycle alone, EFA prices collapsed by ~30%. However, if yield is a criteria, there are other international alternatives, such as IDV.

Alternatives

In my view, the use case for EFA is limited, particularly in today's context. I do not see great growth alpha coming out of Europe and Japan in the next few years. There are far better alternatives if international diversification is the objective.

IPKW improves on EFA by applying a passive yet smart value filter of buyback intensity, capturing cash-rich firms likely to outperform in today’s late-cycle, valuation-conscious market. While EFA offers broad diversification, it feels growth stuck and thematically redundant. IPKW, in contrast, benefits from superior performance in macro conditions like now, where buybacks flourish. Its methodology avoids backward-looking screens and builds a more focused and quality-rich exposure across developed markets. And the outperformance is mostly across all regimes as well, although more so in today's context.

CGDG is another offbeat alternative. Although anchored in the US, it still has large international diversification and should play out well, combining US growth with risk diversification and good yields. CGDG also balances growth with defensives very well and provides a more favorable risk/reward profile for long-term investors.

For income seeking investors, IDVO offers a compelling case. While its overall performance is not far off from EFA, a yield of ~6% is great. And the diversification (including geographical) is far better than seen in the case of EFA - benefits yet to show up in its short tenure so far, but theoretically a stronger play.

Diminished Role In A Shifting Global Landscape

All in all, EFA appears to be an imperfect hedge to the US markets. Its growth prospects are not exciting compared to the US markets. And emerging markets are missing - which is by design, of course. I find some lesser-known names like CGDG, IDVO, and IPKW more favorably placed to meet EFA's objectives, i.e., 1) cushion drawdowns while providing a higher overall return and 2) provide risk diversification beyond the US.

There have been periods in the past where EFA has worked (such as the lost decade), but I don't see markets shaping up for a repeat given structural growth limits in Japan and Europe today compared to two decades ago. And even if such a regime change does happen, there is no hurry. Usually, if such a long-term trend emerges, it will span years, giving sufficient time for investors to switch gears when it happens and not in anticipation. EFA's effectiveness as a short-term hedge in crashes is not great anyway. Either it is missing or the hedge benefits sustain for a very short time before correlations revert.

I rate EFA as a hold. Even among the popular peer set, one can look at IEFA and VEA for lower costs with similar return profiles (with minor theoretical thematic differences and no real return profile differences). With geopolitical instability and tariff wars likely to cut both ways in terms of performance for EFA, investors could wait until more clarity emerges. In a de-globalized world of low correlations, EFA could make sense - if that happens.
